reason: Both papers support the finding that migrating quantization difficulty from activations to weights improves accuracy, with AWQ extending SmoothQuant's activation-smoothing insight to a hardware-friendly weight-only scheme.
GPTQ and LLM.int8() both demonstrate that sub-8-bit post-training quantization of LLMs is feasible without retraining, supporting the broader claim that PTQ can match fine-tuned baselines at scale.

Q-Diffusion finds that uniform quantization schedules used in transformer-focused methods like ZeroQuant degrade diffusion model quality significantly, contradicting the assumption that a single quantization strategy generalizes across architectures.

MQBench and GPTQ address different problem settings — MQBench focuses on reproducible benchmarking of quantization-aware training pipelines, while GPTQ targets one-shot post-training quantization for generative models. Their findings neither reinforce nor contradict each other.

The adversarial quantization attack paper and EfficientDiffusion operate in entirely separate problem domains — one studies robustness under quantization, the other studies generation quality. No direct agreement or disagreement exists between their conclusions.
The quantization survey provides a broad taxonomy of methods including hardware-aware approaches, but does not make specific empirical claims that ZeroQuant's results either confirm or challenge.
